verses.text: After Babel-the-empire of chapter ten, humanity is now united by one shared language. That sounds beautiful — but Genesis is about to show how easily unity can turn against God.
Humanity migrates east and settles in the plain of Shinar — the same land where Nimrod built his empire. The setting matters: this is the cradle of human civilization rebelling.
They invent brick and mortar — new technology in service of a self-made world. The chapter is tracking what humans do when they no longer feel they need God.
Their motive is spelled out: let us make us a name. They want fame, security, and a way up to heaven on their own terms. Pride and self-protection mixed together.
The LORD came down to see — a deliberate Genesis irony. The tower was so high they thought it reached heaven; from heaven God still has to come down to inspect it.
The people are united. Nothing will be restrained from them. Unity without God can magnify human destructiveness — that is why God will divide what they would not divide on their own.

Echoing the let us make of chapter one, God says let us go down. He confounds their language and scatters them across the earth. The judgment is also a mercy — it limits how much damage one united rebellion can do.

Echoing the let us make of chapter one, God says let us go down. He confounds their language and scatters them across the earth. The judgment is also a mercy — it limits how much damage one united rebellion can do.
The city is named Babel — sounding like the Hebrew word for confused. What they tried to make a great name became a name of confusion. Self-made glory always ends here.
Genesis now shifts from the whole human family to one narrowing line. Shem fathered Arphaxad two years after the flood and lived another 500 years. The line is being tracked toward Abram.
Genesis now shifts from the whole human family to one narrowing line. Shem fathered Arphaxad two years after the flood and lived another 500 years. The line is being tracked toward Abram.
Arphaxad fathered Salah at 35 and lived another 403 years. Lifespans are now contracting — no more 900-year men. The world is shrinking back to mortal scale.
Arphaxad fathered Salah at 35 and lived another 403 years. Lifespans are now contracting — no more 900-year men. The world is shrinking back to mortal scale.
Salah fathered Eber at 30 and lived 403 more. Eber is the namesake of the word Hebrew — the patriarch from whom Israel's identity gets its name.
Salah fathered Eber at 30 and lived 403 more. Eber is the namesake of the word Hebrew — the patriarch from whom Israel's identity gets its name.
Eber fathered Peleg at 34 and lived 430 more. Peleg's name marked the division of the earth — chapter ten's hint, now woven into the family record.
Eber fathered Peleg at 34 and lived 430 more. Peleg's name marked the division of the earth — chapter ten's hint, now woven into the family record.
Peleg fathered Reu at 30 and lived 209 more. Halved again from his fathers. The line continues, generations getting shorter.
Peleg fathered Reu at 30 and lived 209 more. Halved again from his fathers. The line continues, generations getting shorter.
Reu fathered Serug at 32, then lived another 207 years. The genealogy moves on, generation by generation, toward someone we haven't met yet.
Reu fathered Serug at 32, then lived another 207 years. The genealogy moves on, generation by generation, toward someone we haven't met yet.
Serug fathered Nahor at 30 and lived another 200 years. Two generations away from Abram, three away from the great call.
Serug fathered Nahor at 30 and lived another 200 years. Two generations away from Abram, three away from the great call.
Nahor fathered Terah at 29 and lived another 119 years. The line is almost at the threshold.
Nahor fathered Terah at 29 and lived another 119 years. The line is almost at the threshold.
Terah, at seventy, fathers Abram, Nahor, and Haran. Abram is named first — though tradition says he was born last. The Bible orders this list around the one who matters most for what's next.
These are the generations of Terah. Haran dies in his homeland Ur of the Chaldeans, leaving his son Lot. Abram and Lot's story will be intertwined for chapters to come.
These are the generations of Terah. Haran dies in his homeland Ur of the Chaldeans, leaving his son Lot. Abram and Lot's story will be intertwined for chapters to come.
Abram and Nahor marry. Abram's wife is Sarai; Nahor's is Milcah. And then the text drops a quiet bombshell: But Sarai was barren; she had no child. The promise to come will require a miracle.
Abram and Nahor marry. Abram's wife is Sarai; Nahor's is Milcah. And then the text drops a quiet bombshell: But Sarai was barren; she had no child. The promise to come will require a miracle.

Terah moves the whole family from Ur toward Canaan, but they settle in Haran. Terah dies there. Notice the journey was started before God ever spoke to Abram — the call in chapter twelve will be God meeting a man already on the road.
